messages.app.name: NodeQL
messages.project.new.title: New Project
messages.runtime.noExecutable: No executable SQL chain found under EXECUTE QUERY.
messages.settings.title: Settings
messages.tutorial.title: Learn NodeQL
messages.tutorial.solveFirst: Solve the short exercise to continue.
messages.tutorial.visual.runHint: NodeQL compiles your connected blocks into SQL.
messages.tutorial.visual.errorHint: Syntax error near COUNT: check the selected columns.
messages.tutorial.visual.syntaxReady: You now know how NodeQL nodes map to SQL syntax.
messages.tutorial.visual.ready: You are ready to build your first visual query.
messages.tutorial.step.1.title: Build SQL without losing sight of SQL
messages.tutorial.step.1.body: NodeQL combines visual blocks with real SQL output. You can learn query structure, experiment locally and inspect every generated statement.
messages.tutorial.step.2.title: Three areas, one workflow
messages.tutorial.step.2.body: Choose blocks on the left, assemble them in the center and inspect SQL plus database results on the right.
messages.tutorial.step.2.question: Where do you assemble a query?
messages.tutorial.step.3.title: Read the blocks from top to bottom
messages.tutorial.step.3.body: A query begins below EXECUTE QUERY. SELECT chooses data, FROM chooses its source and WHERE filters the rows.
messages.tutorial.step.3.question: Which block chooses the table?
messages.tutorial.step.4.title: Shapes reveal valid connections
messages.tutorial.step.4.body: Drag a block near a compatible connector. NodeQL highlights valid targets and preserves the logical SQL order.
messages.tutorial.step.4.question: What should you do when no connector is highlighted?
messages.tutorial.step.4.answer.3: Move it to a compatible position
messages.tutorial.step.5.title: See the SQL before it runs
messages.tutorial.step.5.body: Mount a local SQLite database, review the generated SQL and select Run SQL. Results and understandable errors appear in the lower output area.
messages.tutorial.step.5.question: Where can you verify the generated statement?
messages.tutorial.step.5.answer.2: In the SQL output on the right
messages.tutorial.step.6.title: Plugin shapes communicate their role
messages.tutorial.step.6.body: Statement plugins join a chain, value plugins fit into value positions and container plugins hold nested blocks. Their extension rail identifies them at a glance.
messages.tutorial.step.6.question: Which plugin shape represents a reusable value?
messages.tutorial.step.7.title: A query needs a SQLite database
messages.tutorial.step.7.body: NodeQL works locally with SQLite files. Use Mount .db to choose a .db file. After that NodeQL knows tables and columns and can offer real names in dropdowns.
messages.tutorial.step.7.question: Why do you mount a .db file first?
messages.tutorial.step.7.answer.2: So tables and columns from the database are known
messages.tutorial.step.8.title: SELECT decides which columns you see
messages.tutorial.step.8.body: In the SELECT block you choose one or more columns. Everything means: show every column from the selected table. That is useful while learning; later individual columns are clearer.
messages.tutorial.step.8.question: What does SELECT do?
messages.tutorial.step.9.title: WHERE keeps only matching rows
messages.tutorial.step.9.body: A WHERE block has a column, an operator and a value. Example: film_id = 350. The database checks each row and keeps only rows where the condition is true.
messages.tutorial.step.9.question: Which three parts form a simple WHERE filter?
messages.tutorial.step.10.title: NodeQL explains every block as SQL
messages.tutorial.step.10.body: The SQL output is your checklist. Do table, columns and filters match your intention? If yes, run SQL. If not, change the blocks.
messages.tutorial.step.10.question: Why should you inspect the SQL output?
messages.tutorial.step.10.answer.2: You can see what the blocks mean before running them
messages.tutorial.step.11.title: Errors are hints, not dead ends
messages.tutorial.step.11.body: When SQL fails, check the last change first. Often a table is missing, a column name is wrong or a value needs quotes. Change one block and test again.
messages.tutorial.step.12.title: Next comes the node and syntax tutorial
messages.tutorial.step.12.body: You now know the interface, database, SELECT, FROM, WHERE and SQL output. Next you will learn the node types and how slots, reporters, joins, GROUP BY and HAVING work.
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.1.title: Every node has a role in the query
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.1.body: NodeQL uses starter nodes, statement nodes and value nodes. Starter nodes begin execution, statement nodes form the SQL chain and value nodes fill inputs inside other nodes.
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.2.title: Statement nodes read like SQL clauses
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.2.body: Blocks that stack vertically become the statement chain. Read them from top to bottom: SELECT describes the output, FROM chooses the table and later clauses refine the result.
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.2.question: How do you read a statement chain in NodeQL?
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.3.title: Slots are the editable parts of syntax
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.3.body: A slot is a placeholder inside a node. It can hold a column, text value, number, table name or reporter node, depending on what that SQL clause expects.
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.3.question: What does a slot inside a node represent?
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.3.answer.3: An editable value in the SQL syntax
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.4.title: Reporter nodes return a value
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.4.body: Reporter-style nodes fit into value slots. Examples are COUNT(*), UPPER(name), text literals and date functions. They do not run alone; they become part of a larger clause.
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.4.question: Where does a reporter node belong?
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.5.title: NodeQL protects the SQL clause order
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.5.body: SQL has a logical clause order. NodeQL helps preserve it: SELECT, FROM, JOIN, WHERE and GROUP BY appear in the positions where the database expects them.
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.5.question: Which clause normally chooses the table source?
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.6.title: Plugin nodes follow the same syntax rules
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.6.body: Plugin nodes can be statements, values or containers. Their shape tells you how they connect, and their declared inputs tell you which values are required.
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.6.question: What tells you how a plugin node connects?
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.7.title: SELECT and FROM form the smallest query
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.7.body: SELECT describes the output, FROM describes the source. In NodeQL both are visible as blocks. Once both are correct, the database knows what to show.
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.7.question: Which two parts does a simple table query usually need?
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.8.title: WHERE filters rows before output
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.8.body: WHERE runs before GROUP BY and before the final output. NodeQL splits the condition into column, operator and value so the check stays visible.
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.8.question: When does WHERE apply?
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.8.answer.3: When filtering individual rows before output
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.9.title: JOIN connects tables through matching columns
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.9.body: A JOIN needs a second table and two columns that belong together. In NodeQL you see this as left column = right column.
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.9.question: What does the JOIN condition describe?
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.10.title: GROUP BY creates groups, HAVING filters groups
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.10.body: GROUP BY summarizes rows by a column. COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN and MAX calculate values over these groups. HAVING then checks conditions such as SUM(film_id) = 350.
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.10.question: What does HAVING filter?
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.11.title: ORDER BY sorts the finished result
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.11.body: ORDER BY does not change which rows exist. It only changes their order. Ascending means small to large or A to Z. Descending reverses that.
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.11.question: What does ORDER BY change?
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.12.title: Use nodes as readable SQL pieces
messages.tutorial.syntax.step.12.body: When you add a node, ask what syntax role it plays: starter, statement, slot value, reporter or container. That habit makes NodeQL easier to learn and easier to debug.
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.1.title: Build a complete readable query
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.1.body: Use the intermediate path when SELECT, FROM and WHERE already make sense. You will connect filters, joins, groups and execution checks into one workflow.
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.2.title: Join tables through matching keys
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.2.body: A JOIN adds rows from another table. Keep the source table clear, then connect the join block and define the columns that identify matching records.
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.2.question: What does a JOIN need to combine two tables reliably?
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.3.title: Summarize rows with GROUP BY
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.3.body: Aggregate functions such as COUNT, SUM and AVG summarize rows. GROUP BY decides which rows belong together before the result is shown.
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.3.question: Which part decides the buckets for an aggregate result?
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.4.title: Keep reusable values separate
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.4.body: Parameter-style values make a query easier to review and reuse. Put changing values into value slots instead of hiding them inside long SQL text.
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.4.question: Why should changing values stay in visible value slots?
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.4.answer.1: They are easier to review and replace
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.5.title: Read the SQL before trusting it
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.5.body: Before running a larger query, scan the SQL output. Check table names, join conditions, filters and grouping before you use the result.
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.5.question: What should you verify before running a larger query?
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.6.title: Use plugin blocks by their shape
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.6.body: Plugin blocks follow the same connection rules as built-in blocks. Their shape tells you whether they belong in a statement chain, a value slot or a container.
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.6.question: Where does a value plugin belong?
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.6.answer.2: On top of the title bar
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.7.title: Use error messages as signposts
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.7.body: When a query fails, compare the error with the SQL output. Look for misspelled columns, missing tables, invalid join conditions or values without quotes.
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.7.question: What should you inspect first when a query fails?
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.7.answer.2: The error message and generated SQL
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.8.title: Check aggregate, column, operator and value separately
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.8.body: HAVING is clearer when you think in parts: which function, which column, which comparison, which threshold. Example: SUM(film_id) = 350.
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.8.question: Which parts make a clear HAVING block?
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.9.title: Review plugin blocks before using them
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.9.body: Before adding plugins to a query, read their shape, inputs and description. A value plugin belongs in a slot, a statement plugin in the chain and a container plugin around other blocks.
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.9.question: What tells you where a plugin belongs?
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.10.title: Rebuild a report in small checks
messages.tutorial.intermediate.step.10.body: Pick a report you understand, rebuild it as blocks and compare the generated SQL after each added part. This keeps mistakes visible while the query grows.
messages.tutorial.expert.step.1.title: Treat NodeQL as a visual query layer
messages.tutorial.expert.step.1.body: The advanced path focuses on how blocks, generated SQL, plugin manifests and data-source boundaries fit together. Use it when you already know SQL concepts.
messages.tutorial.expert.step.2.title: Choose joins for intent, not habit
messages.tutorial.expert.step.2.body: INNER, LEFT and other join types express different result guarantees. Select the join that matches the question before tuning columns or filters.
messages.tutorial.expert.step.2.question: What should drive the selected join type?
messages.tutorial.expert.step.3.title: Separate row filters from group filters
messages.tutorial.expert.step.3.body: WHERE filters rows before grouping. HAVING filters grouped results after aggregation. Keeping those roles separate prevents subtle report errors.
messages.tutorial.expert.step.3.question: Which clause filters aggregate groups?
messages.tutorial.expert.step.4.title: Use generated SQL as the debugging contract
messages.tutorial.expert.step.4.body: When a result looks wrong, inspect the SQL first. The generated statement is the common contract between the visual workspace, the runtime and the database.
messages.tutorial.expert.step.4.question: What is the first artifact to inspect when a complex query fails?
messages.tutorial.expert.step.5.title: Respect plugin boundaries
messages.tutorial.expert.step.5.body: A plugin declares its block shape, inputs, data access and host requirements. Review those boundaries before mixing plugin behavior into a query workflow.
messages.tutorial.expert.step.5.question: What should a plugin declare before it is trusted in a workflow?
messages.tutorial.expert.step.6.title: Design queries so assumptions stay visible
messages.tutorial.expert.step.6.body: Expose thresholds, dates and filters as explicit values. That makes reviews easier and reduces accidental changes hidden inside a long statement.
messages.tutorial.expert.step.6.question: Which values should stay visible for review?
messages.tutorial.expert.step.7.title: Review queries like small programs
messages.tutorial.expert.step.7.body: Complex block chains need reviews. Is the source correct? Are joins intentional? Are rows and groups filtered in the right place? Is the result reproducible?
messages.tutorial.expert.step.7.question: Which question belongs in a query review?
messages.tutorial.expert.step.7.answer.1: Are source, joins, filters and result intent clear?
messages.tutorial.expert.step.7.answer.2: Is the window decoration bright enough?
messages.tutorial.expert.step.7.answer.3: Is the project name very long?
messages.tutorial.expert.step.8.title: Separate local SQL logic from plugin behavior
messages.tutorial.expert.step.8.body: Plugins extend NodeQL, but they remain external contracts. Check host requirements, data access and expected inputs before treating plugin results as part of an important query.
messages.tutorial.expert.step.8.question: Why should plugin boundaries stay visible?
messages.tutorial.expert.step.8.answer.2: So data access and inputs remain reviewable
messages.tutorial.expert.step.9.title: Keep assumptions visible and changeable
messages.tutorial.expert.step.9.body: Good NodeQL projects explain themselves: fixed thresholds stay in value slots, important filters are named and the SQL output stays readable enough to discuss.
messages.tutorial.expert.step.9.question: What makes a query more maintainable?
messages.tutorial.expert.step.9.answer.3: Visible values, clear filters and readable SQL
messages.tutorial.expert.step.10.title: Teach the workflow by making intent explicit
messages.tutorial.expert.step.10.body: For advanced work, keep every block chain explainable: source, relation, filter, grouping, output and extension boundary. That discipline makes projects easier for others to learn.
messages.languages.title: Language Packs
messages.languages.notConfigured: Online language catalog is not configured for this build.
messages.plugins.title: NodeQL Plugins
messages.plugins.noneHint: Install a plugin.json manifest to add blocks.
messages.plugins.repository.hint: Add trusted community catalogs. Manifests are verified with SHA-256 before installation.
messages.workspace.deleteRoot.title: Delete root script?
messages.workspace.deleteRoot.message: This will remove the trigger and its attached chain.
